params.text:
Common causes of fire in care settings

Knowing where fires usually start helps you stay alert. In care homes the most frequent causes are:

  • Electrical faults and appliances – the single biggest cause. Overloaded sockets, daisy-chained extension leads, damaged cables, unattended chargers and faulty equipment can all ignite.
  • Cooking – unattended pans, hot fat and grease build-up in kitchens and kitchenettes.
  • Smoking – carelessly discarded cigarettes, smoking in bed or in unauthorised areas, and smoking near oxygen or emollient-soaked bedding.
  • Heaters and naked flames – portable heaters placed too close to curtains, bedding or furniture, and candles.
  • Arson – deliberate fires, which is why rubbish and combustible materials should never be left where they can be set alight.

Good housekeeping

Most fires are preventable. "Good housekeeping" simply means keeping the environment tidy and safe so that fuel and ignition sources are kept apart:

  • Keep corridors, fire exits and stairways clear – never store equipment, laundry trolleys or wheelchairs where they could block an escape route or fuel a fire.
  • Do not overload sockets or use damaged plugs and leads; report faulty electrical equipment and take it out of use.
  • Store flammable liquids, aerosols and clinical waste safely and dispose of rubbish promptly.
  • Switch off and unplug appliances that are not in use where appropriate.

params.question: Which of the following is the most common cause of fire in UK care homes?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Electrical causes – overloaded sockets, faulty appliances and damaged leads – are the leading cause of fire in care homes, which is why reporting faulty equipment matters.
No. Lightning is very rare. Electrical faults and appliances are the leading cause.
No. Electrical faults and appliances are the leading cause of fire in care homes.
No. While it is a theoretical risk, the leading cause is electrical faults and appliances.
